input.prompt: My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?

data.content: My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-04T00:40:07.200Z</current_datetime>

My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?

input.initialPrompt: My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?

data.content:
For waits/blocks under load, collect a **Thread Time (Wall Clock) trace** — it captures both on-CPU and off-CPU (blocked) time, showing where threads wait on locks, I/O, async continuations, etc.

**PerfView command:**

```cmd
PerfView /nogui /accepteula /ThreadTime /zip /merge /maxCollectSec:30 /clrEvents:GC,Contention /providers:"Microsoft-AspNetCore-Server-Kestrel,Microsoft-Extensions-Logging" collect
```

**Key flags explained:**

| Flag | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `/ThreadTime` | Enables CPU **and** wall-clock/blocked-time stacks via context-switch tracing |
| `/maxCollectSec:30` | Limits collection to 30s (adjust to reproduce the slow window) |
| `/clrEvents:GC,Contention` | Captures GC pauses and `Monitor.Enter` lock contention events |
| `/providers:...` | Adds Kestrel + logging EventSource providers for request correlation |
| `/zip /merge` | Merges into a single portable `.etl.zip` |

**Analysis in PerfView:**

1. Open the `.etl.zip` → **Thread Time (with StartStop Activities) Stacks**.
2. Group by `[ASP.NET Core Request]` to see per-request wall-clock breakdowns.
3. Look for large `BLOCKED_TIME`, `DISK_TIME`, or `Contention` frames — those are your bottlenecks.

> **Tip:** If you suspect async starvation (threadpool exhaustion), also add `/threadPoolEvents` and check the **ThreadPool Stacks** view.
data.reasoningText: The user is asking about performance tracing for an ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app on Windows Server experiencing slow requests under load. They want to know which trace to collect and what PerfView arguments to use. This is a knowledge/advice question, not a code task.
